sections.title: When to Use / When NOT to Use
paragraphs: Understanding when a modality is well-suited and when it may be contraindicated is essential for ethical, effective practice.

subsections.title: When to Consider This Approach
items: Substance use disorders and addictive behaviors -- strongest evidence base
Health behavior change (medication adherence, diet, exercise, chronic disease)
Pre-contemplation and contemplation stages -- clients ambivalent about change
Dual diagnosis -- combining MI with other treatments for co-occurring disorders
As a prelude to other therapies -- building motivation before CBT, EMDR, etc.
Adolescents and mandated clients who may be resistant to treatment

subsections.title: When NOT to Use (or Use with Caution)
items: Clients already in the action stage who need skill-building, not motivation
Acute psychiatric crisis requiring immediate stabilization
When the client needs psychoeducation before making informed decisions
Severe cognitive impairment limiting reflective conversation
When there is no genuine ambivalence -- the client needs tools, not motivation

narrative.name: Narrative Therapy
narrative.description: Re-author life stories by externalizing problems and discovering alternative narratives.

sections.title: Overview
paragraphs:
Narrative Therapy is a respectful, non-blaming approach to counseling that views people as the experts in their 
            own lives. Developed by Michael White and David Epston, this therapy centers on the stories we tell about ourselves 
            and our experiences, recognizing that these narratives shape our identity and influence our actions.

The approach is based on the idea that problems are separate from people and that individuals have many skills, 
            competencies, beliefs, values, and abilities that will assist them in changing their relationship with problems. 
            Narrative therapy helps people re-author their life stories by externalizing problems and discovering alternative, 
            preferred narratives.

subsections.title: Foundational Principles
items:
People Are Not Problems
                
                  The person is never the problem; the problem is the problem. This fundamental principle separates people's 
                  identities from the issues they face, reducing shame and blame while opening space for change.

Client as Expert
                
                  Clients are viewed as experts in their own lives. The therapist takes a curious, not-knowing stance, 
                  learning from the client rather than imposing expert knowledge. This collaborative approach respects 
                  client autonomy and wisdom.

Stories Shape Reality
                
                  The narratives we construct about our lives influence how we see ourselves and what actions we take. 
                  By changing our stories, we can change our lived experience and create new possibilities.

Context Matters
                
                  Problems don't exist in a vacuum but are influenced by social, cultural, and political contexts. 
                  Understanding these contexts helps identify how dominant cultural narratives may contribute to personal struggles.

sections.title: Case Conceptualization Lens
paragraphs:
Narrative therapy frames problems as separate from people and focuses on how dominant stories shape identity. The lens highlights
            language, cultural context, and unique outcomes that support preferred narratives.
items: Dominant story and problem-saturated language
Effects of the problem on life and relationships
Unique outcomes and exceptions
Cultural discourses influencing meaning
Thin descriptions reinforce stuck identity
Internalized labels limit options
Problem story crowds out alternatives
Externalization of the problem
Re-authoring preferred narratives
Thickening exceptions with meaning
Externalizing conversations
Mapping the influence of the problem
Re-membering and documentation practices
Tree of Life exercises
sections.callout:
Example formulation: The client's "I'm a failure" story shapes avoidance and isolation. Narrative work
              externalizes the problem and identifies times of persistence to build a preferred identity of resilience.

sections.title: Cultural & Context Lens
paragraphs:
Integrate cultural identity, systemic context, and lived experience into formulation. Explore how power, oppression,
            and community shape presenting concerns and change pathways.

Family norms, community supports, cultural values

Use brief measures and session feedback to track progress and adjust the plan. Document baseline, monitor change,

sections.title: Core Concepts and Techniques
items:
Externalization
              
                The central technique of separating the person from the problem. Rather than saying "I am depressed," 
                the client learns to say "Depression is affecting my life." This linguistic shift creates space between 
                the person and the problem, reducing self-blame and opening possibilities for action. Externalization 
                helps clients view problems as external entities that can be examined, questioned, and resisted rather 
                than as inherent character flaws.
                
                  Externalization Questions:
                  • "When did Anxiety first show up in your life?"
                  • "What does Depression tell you about yourself?"
                  • "How does the Problem try to convince you to give up?"
                  • "What tactics does Anger use to take over?"

Unique Outcomes
              
                Identifying times when the problem could have dominated but didn't—exceptions to the problem-saturated story. 
                These "sparkling moments" or "unique outcomes" provide evidence that the dominant negative narrative isn't 
                the whole truth. They reveal client strengths, resources, and alternative possibilities. By exploring these 
                exceptions, clients can build alternative, preferred stories about themselves.
                
                  Unique Outcome Questions:
                  • "Can you tell me about a time when you stood up to the Problem?"
                  • "When has Anxiety tried to take over but you didn't let it?"
                  • "What was different about that time?"
                  • "How did you manage to do that?"

Re-authoring Conversations
              
                Collaborative dialogues that help clients develop new, preferred narratives about their lives. Through 
                careful questioning, the therapist helps clients connect unique outcomes into a new storyline that reflects 
                their values, hopes, and preferred identity. This process involves thickening the alternative story by 
                exploring its history, meaning, and implications for the future.

Deconstruction
              
                Examining and questioning taken-for-granted beliefs, assumptions, and dominant cultural narratives that 
                may be contributing to problems. This involves exploring where beliefs came from, whose interests they 
                serve, and whether they're helpful. Deconstruction reveals that many "truths" are actually socially 
                constructed and can be challenged.
                
                  Deconstructive Questions:
                  • "Where did you learn that belief?"
                  • "Who benefits from this way of thinking?"
                  • "Is this belief serving you well?"
                  • "What would happen if you questioned this assumption?"

Re-membering Conversations
              
                Exploring significant relationships and their influence on identity. This involves identifying people 
                (living or deceased) who have contributed positively to the client's life and values. Re-membering 
                conversations help clients recognize they're not alone and that their preferred identity has been shaped 
                by meaningful relationships. This creates a sense of connection and support.

Witnessing and Outsider Witnesses
              
                Inviting others to witness and acknowledge the client's preferred story. This might involve family members, 
                friends, or a reflecting team. Witnesses share what resonated with them, what they learned, and how the 
                story connects to their own experiences. This validation strengthens the alternative narrative and creates 
                a sense of community around the new story.

Definitional Ceremonies
              
                Structured rituals where the client's preferred story is witnessed, acknowledged, and celebrated by a 
                community. These ceremonies mark transitions and solidify new identities. They provide public recognition 
                of change and create accountability for maintaining the new narrative.

sections.title: Four-Phase Therapeutic Process
paragraphs: Narrative therapy typically follows a four-phase process, though these phases often overlap and cycle:
items:
Deconstruction Phase
              
                Focus on separating the person from their problem and exploring how problems have shaped their life narrative. 
                The therapist uses externalization to create distance from problem-saturated stories. This phase involves 
                mapping the influence of the problem on the person's life, relationships, and identity. Questions explore 
                how the problem operates, what tactics it uses, and how it has affected various life domains. The goal is 
                to objectify the problem so it can be examined and challenged rather than accepted as truth.

Unique Outcomes Phase
              
                Identify "unique outcomes" or narrative fragments—events that challenge the dominant negative narrative. 
                These are times when the client acted in ways that contradict the problem story, demonstrating strength, 
                competence, or values. The therapist helps clients discover moments when they resisted the problem, made 
                different choices, or experienced life differently. These exceptions provide evidence for an alternative 
                story and reveal client resources and capabilities that may have been overlooked.

Re-authoring Phase
              
                Collaborate with clients to reconstruct their life narrative in a more empowering way. Using unique outcomes 
                as building blocks, the therapist helps clients develop a new, preferred storyline that reflects their values, 
                hopes, and preferred identity. This involves "thickening" the alternative story by exploring its history 
                (past examples), landscape of action (what happened), and landscape of identity (what it means about who 
                they are). The new story is developed through open dialogues that create meaning from previously marginalized 
                experiences.

Certification/Validation Phase
              
                Validate the new, preferred narrative and strengthen the client's commitment to it. This often involves 
                inviting witnesses or support systems to acknowledge the new story, creating definitional ceremonies, or 
                documenting the new narrative in letters or certificates. The therapist helps the client anticipate how 
                they'll maintain the new story when challenges arise. This phase reinforces the client's agency and expertise 
                in their own life, celebrating the journey and the new identity that has emerged.

sections.title: Tree of Life Exercise
paragraphs:
The Tree of Life is a therapeutic exercise rooted in narrative therapy that helps individuals reclaim and 
            reframe their personal identity and life story. It provides a hopeful and inspiring approach for processing 
            life experiences, particularly useful for children, youth, and adults who have experienced challenges.

subsections.title: Components of the Tree
items:
Roots
                
                  Represent personal history, cultural background, and origins. Where you come from, your ancestors, 
                  your heritage, and the people and places that have shaped you. The roots ground you and provide 
                  nourishment for growth.
                  
                    Questions: Where were you born? Who are your ancestors? What cultural traditions are important to you?

Ground
                
                  Represents your current life context—where you live now, your daily activities, and your present 
                  circumstances. The ground provides stability and connection to the here and now.
                  
                    Questions: Where do you live? What do you do each day? What is your current situation?

Trunk
                
                  Represents personal strengths, skills, values, and identity. The solid core of who you are—your 
                  abilities, talents, and the qualities that make you unique. The trunk is strong and supports 
                  everything else.
                  
                    Questions: What are you good at? What are your strengths? What values guide you? What makes you unique?

Branches
                
                  Represent future goals, hopes, dreams, and aspirations. Where you want to go, what you want to 
                  achieve, and the possibilities that lie ahead. Branches reach upward and outward toward the future.
                  
                    Questions: What are your dreams? What do you hope for? What goals do you have? Where do you see yourself going?

Leaves
                
                  Represent important relationships and support systems. The people who matter to you—family, friends, 
                  mentors, community members. Leaves provide beauty and help the tree breathe and grow.
                  
                    Questions: Who is important to you? Who supports you? Who do you care about? Who has helped you?

Fruits
                
                  Represent gifts you've received from others—lessons learned, wisdom gained, and positive influences. 
                  The fruits are the nourishment you've received that helps you grow.
                  
                    Questions: What have others taught you? What gifts have you received? What wisdom guides you?

Storms
                
                  Represent challenges, difficulties, and problems faced. Rather than being part of the tree itself, 
                  storms are external forces that the tree weathers. This externalization separates problems from identity.
                  
                    Questions: What challenges have you faced? What difficulties have you weathered? How has the tree survived storms?

subsections.title: Therapeutic Benefits
items: Provides a visual, creative method for exploring identity and life story
Externalizes problems (storms) from the person (tree)
Highlights strengths, resources, and support systems
Connects past, present, and future in a coherent narrative
Offers hope by emphasizing growth and resilience
Particularly effective for trauma survivors and those who have experienced loss
Can be done individually or in groups
Accessible for all ages and cultural backgrounds

sections.title: Therapeutic Question Banks
paragraphs:
Narrative therapy relies heavily on skillful questioning to help clients explore and reconstruct their stories. 
            Here are key question types and examples:

subsections.title: Externalization Questions

sfbt.name: Solution-Focused Brief Therapy
sfbt.description: Build solutions and leverage strengths through future-focused, goal-oriented questions.

sections.title: Overview
paragraphs:
Solution-Focused Brief Therapy (SFBT) is a short-term, goal-focused, evidence-based therapeutic approach that 
            emphasizes building solutions rather than solving problems. Developed by Steve de Shazer and Insoo Kim Berg at 
            the Brief Family Therapy Center in Milwaukee, SFBT focuses on what clients want to achieve rather than on the 
            problems that brought them to therapy.

SFBT is based on the premise that clients have the strengths and resources needed to create change in their lives. 
            Rather than dwelling on problems and their causes, SFBT helps clients envision their preferred future and identify 
            the steps needed to get there. The approach is collaborative, respectful, and empowering, typically requiring 
            5-8 sessions.

subsections.title: Core Assumptions
items:
Focus on Solutions, Not Problems
                
                  Talking about solutions and desired futures is more productive than analyzing problems and their causes. 
                  The therapy concentrates on what's working and what clients want, rather than what's wrong and why.

Clients Are the Experts
                
                  Clients know what's best for them and have the resources and strengths to create change. The therapist's 
                  role is to facilitate the discovery and amplification of these existing resources, not to provide expert 
                  solutions.

Small Changes Lead to Bigger Changes
                
                  Small, incremental changes can create ripple effects that lead to larger transformations. SFBT focuses 
                  on identifying and building upon small successes rather than attempting dramatic overhauls.

If It Works, Do More of It
                
                  When something is working, even a little bit, do more of it. SFBT identifies what's already working in 
                  clients' lives and helps them amplify these successful strategies.

If It's Not Working, Do Something Different
                
                  When strategies aren't producing desired results, it's time to try something new. SFBT encourages 
                  experimentation and flexibility rather than persisting with ineffective approaches.

sections.title: Case Conceptualization Lens
paragraphs:
SFBT conceptualizes change through goals, exceptions, and resources. The lens focuses on the client's preferred future and what
            already works, then builds small, observable steps forward.
items: Preferred future and goal clarity
Exceptions to the problem
Strengths, resources, supports
Progress markers (scaling)
Problem-focused attention crowds out exceptions
Goals stay vague or overly large
Resources remain underused
Amplify exceptions and strengths
Define small, measurable next steps
Track progress and adjust
Miracle question
Scaling and coping questions
Exception-finding
Compliments and strengths feedback
sections.callout:
Example formulation: The client notices calmer evenings when they take a short walk. SFBT targets the
              exception, scales progress, and builds a small routine that supports the preferred future.

sections.title: Cultural & Context Lens
paragraphs:
Integrate cultural identity, systemic context, and lived experience into formulation. Explore how power, oppression,
            and community shape presenting concerns and change pathways.
items: Identity, language, spirituality, migration history
Family norms, community supports, cultural values
Experiences of discrimination or marginalization
Access to resources and barriers to care
Systemic stressors and chronic invalidation
Values-context mismatch or role strain
Stigma, secrecy, or limited access to support
Culturally congruent goals and language
Community connection and support
Empowerment and agency within context
Use culturally relevant metaphors
Collaborate on family/community involvement
Address systemic barriers in treatment plans

sections.title: Measurement-Based Care
paragraphs: Key instruments: SRS (cutoff < 36 = alliance concern), ORS (cutoff < 25 = clinical distress), Scaling Questions (1-10). Administer ORS at start and SRS at end of every session.

Use brief measures and session feedback to track progress and adjust the plan. Document baseline, monitor change,
            and respond early when progress stalls.
items: PHQ-9 (depression) and GAD-7 (anxiety)
Optional: trauma, substance use, or sleep measures
Review trends every 2-4 sessions
Alliance: felt understood and respected
Goals: session fit and clarity
Approach: methods were helpful
Overall: usefulness of the session
No improvement by session 4-6 → adjust plan
Worsening scores → increase support
Stalled progress → change target or modality

sections.title: Core Philosophy and Principles
items:
Change Is Constant and Certain
              
                Change is always happening and is inevitable. Rather than trying to create change from scratch, SFBT helps 
                clients notice and amplify changes that are already occurring. This principle instills hope and recognizes 
                that clients have the capacity to create positive transformations in their lives.

Client Autonomy and Self-Determination
              
                Clients are the experts in their own lives and must want to change. The therapist doesn't dictate solutions 
                but guides clients to discover their own answers. This respect for autonomy creates a collaborative partnership 
                and increases client investment in the change process.

Strength-Based Approach
              
                Identifies and builds on client's existing strengths, resources, and capabilities. Focuses on what clients 
                can do rather than what they cannot do. This positive psychology approach emphasizes resilience, competence, 
                and potential rather than deficits and pathology.

Solution-Building vs. Problem-Solving
              
                Concentrates on constructing solutions rather than deconstructing problems. Encourages hope and possibility 
                by focusing on the preferred future. Helps clients envision and work towards desired outcomes rather than 
                getting stuck in problem analysis.

Goal-Oriented and Time-Limited
              
                Short-term, focused therapeutic method that aims to help clients achieve specific, actionable goals. 
                Typically requires 5-8 sessions. Incorporates positive psychology practices and emphasizes efficiency 
                and effectiveness.

Future-Focused Orientation
              
                Emphasizes the future rather than the past. While acknowledging that past experiences matter, SFBT focuses 
                on where clients want to go rather than where they've been. This forward-looking perspective creates hope 
                and motivation for change.

Exceptions Are Key
              
                Problems don't happen all the time. Identifying times when the problem doesn't occur (exceptions) reveals 
                existing solutions and client strengths. These exceptions provide templates for creating more of what works.

sections.title: Six-Step SFBT Process
paragraphs:
SFBT typically follows a structured six-step process that guides the therapeutic conversation from problem 
            presentation to solution construction:
items:
Describe the Problem
              
                Begin by briefly exploring the current problem and what brought the client to therapy. However, unlike 
                traditional approaches, SFBT doesn't dwell on problem analysis. The focus is on understanding what the 
                client wants to be different, not on extensive problem exploration. This phase also includes identifying 
                pre-session changes—improvements that have occurred between making the appointment and the first session.

Develop Well-Formed Goals
              
                Help clients construct clear, specific, achievable goals that are stated in positive terms (what they want, 
                not what they don't want). Goals should be concrete, behavioral, and meaningful to the client. Use the 
                miracle question and other techniques to help clients articulate their preferred future. Well-formed goals 
                are: stated in positive terms, specific and concrete, achievable and realistic, important to the client, 
                and described in behavioral terms.

Explore Exceptions
              
                Identify times when the problem doesn't occur or is less severe—these are exceptions. Explore what's different 
                about these times, what the client is doing differently, and what others notice. Exceptions reveal existing 
                solutions and client strengths. They provide evidence that change is possible and offer templates for creating 
                more of what works. Deliberate exceptions (when the client intentionally does something different) are 
                particularly valuable.

Use Scaling Questions
              
                Implement numerical scales (typically 0-10) to measure progress, confidence, and motivation. Scaling questions 
                allow clients to rate their current situation, track improvement, and identify next steps. They make abstract 
                concepts concrete and measurable. Scales can assess: current progress toward goals, confidence in ability to 
                change, importance of the goal, relationship satisfaction, and any other relevant dimension.

Identify Strengths and Resources
              
                Highlight the client's existing strengths, resources, past successes, and coping mechanisms. Help clients 
                recognize their capabilities and the resources available to them. This builds confidence and self-efficacy. 
                Explore: What has worked before? What are you good at? Who can support you? What resources do you have? 
                What have you learned from past challenges?

End with Compliments and Tasks
              
                Conclude sessions with genuine compliments that highlight client strengths and progress. Assign tasks or 
                experiments that help clients do more of what works or try something different. Tasks are collaborative 
                and based on what emerged in the session. They might include: observing what's working, doing more of what 
                works, trying something different, or noticing small changes. The therapist takes a brief break before ending 
                to formulate compliments and tasks.

sections.title: Key SFBT Question Types
paragraphs:
The signature technique of SFBT that helps clients envision their preferred future without being constrained 
              by current limitations. It invites clients to imagine how their life would be different if the problem was 
              suddenly solved.

Questions that identify times when the problem doesn't occur or is less severe, revealing existing solutions 
              and client strengths.

subsections.title: The Miracle Question
paragraphs:
The signature technique of SFBT that helps clients envision their preferred future without being constrained 
              by current limitations. It invites clients to imagine how their life would be different if the problem was 
              suddenly solved.

subsections.title: Exception Questions
items: "When doesn't the problem happen?"
"Tell me about a time when you expected the problem to occur but it didn't."
"What's different about the times when the problem is less severe?"
"What are you doing differently when the problem isn't there?"
"How did you make that happen?"
"What would it take to make that happen more often?"
paragraphs:
Questions that identify times when the problem doesn't occur or is less severe, revealing existing solutions 
              and client strengths.

subsections.title: Coping Questions
items: "How have you managed to cope with this?"
"How have you kept things from getting worse?"
"What keeps you going?"
"Where do you find the strength to deal with this?"
"How have you survived this?"
"What stops you from giving up?"
paragraphs:
Questions that highlight client resilience and strength, particularly useful when clients feel overwhelmed 
              or hopeless.

subsections.title: Relationship Questions
items: "What would your best friend say you're good at?"
"If I asked your partner, what would they say is better?"
"What would your children notice is different after the miracle?"
"Who would be the first to notice you're making progress?"
"What would they see you doing differently?"
paragraphs:
Questions that explore how others perceive the client and the situation, providing different perspectives 
              and highlighting strengths.

subsections.title: Pre-Session Change Questions
items: "What's better since you made the appointment?"
"Many people notice that things improve between making an appointment and coming in. What have you noticed?"
"What's different now compared to when you called?"
"How did you make that happen?"
paragraphs:
Questions asked in the first session to identify improvements that occurred between scheduling and attending 
              the appointment.

sections.title: Scaling Questions and Tools
paragraphs:
Scaling questions are a cornerstone of SFBT, using numerical scales (typically 0-10) to make abstract concepts 
            concrete and measurable. They help clients assess their situation, track progress, and identify next steps.
Scales typically use 0-10 where:

subsections.title: Basic Scaling Structure
items: 0 or 1 = The worst the situation could be (or lowest level)
10 = The ideal situation or goal achieved (or highest level)
Current number = Where the client is right now
paragraphs: Scales typically use 0-10 where:

subsections.title: Benefits of Scaling Questions
items:
Makes Progress Visible
                
                  Helps clients recognize small improvements they might otherwise overlook. Even moving from 2 to 3 is 
                  progress worth acknowledging and building upon.

Identifies Next Steps
                
                  Asking "What would be different at [number + 1]?" helps clients identify concrete, achievable next steps 
                  rather than feeling overwhelmed by the distance to 10.

Highlights Strengths
                
                  Asking "What tells you you're at [number] and not lower?" helps clients recognize what they're already 
                  doing well and the resources they're using.

Tracks Change Over Time
                
                  Using the same scale across sessions provides a simple way to monitor progress and celebrate improvements.

Facilitates Communication
                
                  Provides a common language for discussing abstract concepts like confidence, motivation, and relationship 
                  quality. Makes it easier for clients to express complex feelings.

subsections.title: When to Consider This Approach
items: Brief treatment contexts -- designed for 3-8 sessions

dbt.name: Dialectical Behavior Therapy
dbt.description: Balances acceptance and change through skills training, mindfulness, distress tolerance, emotion regulation, and interpersonal effectiveness.

sections.title: Overview
paragraphs: Dialectical Behavior Therapy (DBT) was developed by Marsha Linehan in the late 1980s, originally for chronically suicidal individuals with borderline personality disorder (BPD). It integrates cognitive-behavioral techniques with Zen mindfulness practices and dialectical philosophy.

The core dialectic is between acceptance and change: the therapist simultaneously validates the client's experience while pushing for behavioral change. DBT is structured around four modules of skills training: mindfulness, distress tolerance, emotion regulation, and interpersonal effectiveness.

Standard DBT includes four treatment modes: individual therapy, skills group, phone coaching, and therapist consultation team. The biosocial theory posits that emotional dysregulation arises from a biologically sensitive temperament interacting with an invalidating environment.

sections.title: Case Conceptualization Lens
paragraphs: DBT conceptualizes problems through the biosocial model: emotional vulnerability (biological sensitivity, high reactivity, slow return to baseline) meets an invalidating environment (dismissing, punishing, or intermittently reinforcing emotional expression). This transaction produces pervasive emotion dysregulation.
items: Biological sensitivity to emotional stimuli
History of invalidating environment
Emotion dysregulation across domains (behavioral, cognitive, interpersonal, self)
Skills deficits in mindfulness, distress tolerance, emotion regulation, interpersonal effectiveness
Behavioral patterns: emotion-driven behaviors, crisis-generating behaviors, active passivity, apparent competence
Dialectical dilemmas: emotional vulnerability vs. self-invalidation, active passivity vs. apparent competence, unrelenting crisis vs. inhibited grieving
Chain analysis: prompting event → vulnerability factors → links (thoughts, emotions, behaviors) → problem behavior → consequences
Target hierarchy: life-threatening behaviors, therapy-interfering behaviors, quality-of-life behaviors, skills acquisition
sections.callout: Example formulation: When criticized (prompting event), the client's emotional vulnerability (high sensitivity + slow return to baseline) interacts with learned self-invalidation, producing intense shame that escalates to self-harm (problem behavior). The short-term relief (emotion regulation) reinforces the behavior, while the long-term cost is relationship damage and hospitalization. DBT targets building distress tolerance and emotion regulation skills while validating the pain that drives the behavior.

sections.title: Cultural & Context Lens
paragraphs: DBT has been adapted across cultures and populations. The concept of an 'invalidating environment' must be understood within cultural context — what constitutes invalidation varies across cultures, families, and communities.

Clinicians should consider how cultural norms around emotional expression, help-seeking, and interpersonal relationships interact with DBT's skills-based approach. The dialectical framework can be particularly useful for navigating cultural tensions between individual autonomy and collectivist values.
items: Assess how cultural background shapes emotional expression norms
Consider systemic invalidation (racism, discrimination) as part of the biosocial model
Adapt skills language to be culturally resonant
Recognize that mindfulness practices may align with or conflict with spiritual traditions
Consider accessibility barriers to the full DBT model (group, individual, phone coaching)

sections.title: Measurement-Based Care
paragraphs: DBT uses structured tracking to monitor treatment targets and skills use.

subsections.title: Recommended Instruments
items: DBT Diary Card — Daily tracking of emotions, urges, self-harm, substance use, and skills used. Reviewed at each individual session.
Difficulties in Emotion Regulation Scale (DERS) — 36-item self-report. Scores >80 suggest clinically significant dysregulation. Administer monthly.
Borderline Symptom List (BSL-23) — 23 items measuring BPD symptom severity. Scores >2.0 indicate high severity. Administer monthly.
PHQ-9 — Depression screening. Scores ≥10 suggest moderate depression. Administer every 2-4 weeks.
Columbia Suicide Severity Rating Scale (C-SSRS) — Structured suicide risk assessment. Administer at intake and as clinically indicated.
DBT Ways of Coping Checklist (DBT-WCCL) — Measures use of DBT skills vs. dysfunctional coping. Administer monthly.

subsections.title: Mindfulness
paragraphs: The foundation of all DBT skills. Teaches observing, describing, and participating in the present moment without judgment.
items: Wise Mind — Integration of emotion mind and reasonable mind
What Skills — Observe, Describe, Participate
How Skills — Non-judgmentally, One-mindfully, Effectively

subsections.title: Distress Tolerance
paragraphs: Skills for surviving crisis without making things worse. Includes both crisis survival and reality acceptance skills.
items: TIPP — Temperature, Intense exercise, Paced breathing, Progressive relaxation
STOP — Stop, Take a step back, Observe, Proceed mindfully
Pros and Cons of tolerating vs. not tolerating distress
IMPROVE the Moment — Imagery, Meaning, Prayer, Relaxation, One thing, Vacation, Encouragement
Radical Acceptance — Fully accepting reality as it is
Turning the Mind — Choosing to accept again and again
Willingness vs. Willfulness

subsections.title: Emotion Regulation
paragraphs: Skills for understanding, reducing vulnerability to, and changing unwanted emotions.
items: ABC PLEASE — Accumulate positives, Build mastery, Cope ahead, treat PhysicaL illness, balance Eating, avoid mood-Altering substances, balance Sleep, get Exercise
Check the Facts — Examining whether emotional response fits the facts
Opposite Action — Acting opposite to the emotion's action urge when the emotion doesn't fit the facts
Problem Solving — When the emotion fits the facts, solve the problem
Model of Emotions — Understanding the components of emotional experience

subsections.title: Interpersonal Effectiveness
paragraphs: Skills for maintaining self-respect, building relationships, and getting needs met.
items: DEAR MAN — Describe, Express, Assert, Reinforce, Mindful, Appear confident, Negotiate (objective effectiveness)
GIVE — Gentle, Interested, Validate, Easy manner (relationship effectiveness)
FAST — Fair, no Apologies, Stick to values, Truthful (self-respect effectiveness)
Dialectics in relationships — Walking the middle path
Validation skills — Six levels of validation

sections.title: Key DBT Techniques
items: Chain Analysis — Detailed behavioral analysis of a problem behavior episode, identifying vulnerability factors, prompting events, links, and consequences
Solution Analysis — Generating and evaluating alternative responses at each link in the chain
Validation — Six levels: being present, accurate reflection, mind-reading, understanding in terms of history, understanding in terms of current context, radical genuineness
Dialectical Strategies — Balancing acceptance and change, using metaphor, devil's advocate, extending, making lemonade out of lemons
Commitment Strategies — Foot in the door, door in the face, connecting to values, cheerleading, devil's advocate
Exposure-Based Procedures — Opposite action as a form of emotional exposure
Cognitive Modification — Checking the facts, dialectical thinking
Contingency Management — Reinforcing adaptive behavior, extinguishing maladaptive behavior

sections.title: When to Use / When NOT to Use

subsections.title: Best Fit
items: Borderline personality disorder
Chronic suicidality and self-harm
Emotion dysregulation across diagnoses
Substance use disorders with emotional dysregulation
Eating disorders (DBT-adapted)
Treatment-resistant depression
PTSD with concurrent self-harm (DBT PE)

subsections.title: Cautions / Contraindications
items: Active psychosis without stabilization
Severe cognitive impairment limiting skills acquisition
Client unwilling to commit to treatment structure (diary cards, skills group)
When a less intensive intervention would suffice
When the primary issue is a specific phobia or circumscribed anxiety (standard CBT may be more efficient)

sections.title: References

Linehan, M. M. (2015). DBT Skills Training Manual (2nd ed.). Guilford Press.
Linehan, M. M. (2020). Building a Life Worth Living: A Memoir. Random House.
Chapman, A. L. (2006). Dialectical behavior therapy: Current indications and unique elements. Psychiatry, 3(9), 62-68.
Koerner, K. (2012). Doing Dialectical Behavior Therapy: A Practical Guide. Guilford Press.

eft.name: Emotionally Focused Therapy
eft.description: Transforms distressed relationships by identifying and reshaping negative interaction cycles through the lens of attachment theory.

sections.title: Overview
paragraphs: Emotionally Focused Therapy (EFT) was developed by Sue Johnson and Les Greenberg in the 1980s. It is grounded in attachment theory (Bowlby) and humanistic/experiential therapy traditions. EFT is primarily known as a couples therapy but has been adapted for individuals (EFIT) and families (EFFT).

The core premise is that emotional disconnection and insecure attachment bonds underlie relationship distress. When attachment needs for safety, comfort, and connection are unmet, partners fall into rigid negative interaction cycles (pursue-withdraw, attack-attack, withdraw-withdraw).

EFT follows a structured three-stage, nine-step process: de-escalation of negative cycles, restructuring interactions through accessing deeper attachment emotions, and consolidation of new bonding patterns.

sections.title: Case Conceptualization Lens
paragraphs: EFT conceptualizes relationship distress as a protest response to perceived attachment insecurity. Partners develop rigid interaction patterns (negative cycles) that are maintained by secondary reactive emotions (anger, numbness) masking primary attachment emotions (fear, sadness, longing).
items: Identify the negative interaction cycle (e.g., pursue-withdraw)
Map each partner's position in the cycle
Identify secondary reactive emotions (anger, frustration, shutdown)
Access primary attachment emotions (fear of abandonment, longing for connection, sadness about disconnection)
Identify attachment needs and fears driving each partner's position
Assess attachment history and working models
Track the cycle's triggers and escalation patterns
Identify blocks to emotional engagement (attachment injuries, trauma)
sections.callout: Example formulation: When Partner A perceives emotional distance (trigger), attachment fear activates ('I'm not important to you'), producing anxiety that drives pursuing behavior (criticism, demands). Partner B, feeling attacked, experiences shame and fear of failure, withdrawing to self-protect. The withdrawal confirms Partner A's fear, intensifying pursuit. The cycle — not either partner — is the enemy. EFT targets accessing the underlying attachment emotions and needs to create new bonding interactions.

sections.title: Cultural & Context Lens
paragraphs: Attachment is considered a universal human need, but its expression varies significantly across cultures. EFT clinicians must be sensitive to cultural norms around emotional expression, gender roles in relationships, and definitions of intimacy.

The model has been applied across diverse populations including same-sex couples, military couples, and culturally diverse dyads. However, the emphasis on emotional vulnerability and direct expression may need adaptation for cultures that value emotional restraint.
items: Assess cultural norms around emotional expression between partners
Consider how gender role expectations shape the negative cycle
Explore culturally specific attachment behaviors and meanings
Adapt the pace of emotional deepening to cultural comfort
Consider power dynamics related to cultural, racial, or socioeconomic differences between partners
Recognize that 'withdraw' may be culturally normative rather than avoidant in some contexts

sections.title: Measurement-Based Care
paragraphs: EFT uses relationship-focused measures to track treatment progress.

subsections.title: Recommended Instruments
items: Dyadic Adjustment Scale (DAS) — 32-item measure of relationship satisfaction. Scores <100 suggest significant distress. Administer monthly.
Experiences in Close Relationships (ECR-R) — 36-item attachment style measure. Tracks changes in attachment anxiety and avoidance. Administer at intake and every 2-3 months.
Couple Satisfaction Index (CSI-16) — 16-item relationship satisfaction measure. Scores <51.5 indicate notable dissatisfaction. Administer every 2-4 weeks.
Relationship Assessment Scale (RAS) — 7-item brief satisfaction measure. Scores <3.5 suggest dissatisfaction. Administer every 2-4 weeks.
Session Rating Scale (SRS) — 4-item alliance measure. Administer at end of each session.
EFT Therapist Fidelity Scale — Used in supervision to track adherence to the EFT model.

subsections.title: Stage 1: De-escalation (Steps 1-4)
paragraphs: The goal is to identify and de-escalate the negative interaction cycle.
items: Step 1: Identify relational conflict issues
Step 2: Identify the negative interaction cycle
Step 3: Access unacknowledged emotions underlying positions
Step 4: Reframe the problem in terms of the cycle, attachment needs, and fears

subsections.title: Stage 2: Restructuring (Steps 5-7)
paragraphs: The goal is to create new interactions based on deeper emotional engagement.
items: Step 5: Promote identification with disowned attachment needs and fears
Step 6: Promote acceptance of partner's experience and new interaction patterns
Step 7: Facilitate the expression of attachment needs and bonding events (key change events: withdrawer re-engagement and blamer softening)

subsections.title: Stage 3: Consolidation (Steps 8-9)
paragraphs: The goal is to consolidate new positions and cycles of attachment security.
items: Step 8: Facilitate new solutions to old problems
Step 9: Consolidate new positions and new cycles of attachment security

sections.title: Key EFT Techniques
items: Reflection — Mirroring and validating each partner's emotional experience
Validation — Normalizing emotions within the attachment framework
Evocative Responding — Using questions and reflections to deepen emotional experience
Heightening — Repeating, using imagery, or slowing down to amplify key emotional moments
Empathic Conjecture — Tentatively suggesting what a partner might be feeling at a deeper level
Tracking and Reflecting the Cycle — Naming the pattern as it happens in session
Reframing — Placing behaviors in the context of attachment needs and fears
Restructuring Interactions — Choreographing new enactments where partners share vulnerable emotions directly
Enactments — Facilitating in-session interactions where partners turn to each other with newly accessed emotions

sections.title: When to Use / When NOT to Use

subsections.title: Best Fit
items: Couple distress with pursue-withdraw or withdraw-withdraw cycles
Attachment injuries (affairs, betrayals, emotional abandonment)
Couples affected by depression, anxiety, or PTSD
Military couples and trauma-affected relationships
Same-sex and diverse couple configurations
Individual attachment-related difficulties (EFIT)
Family relationship repair (EFFT)

subsections.title: Cautions / Contraindications
items: Active domestic violence with a pattern of coercive control (safety first)
Active untreated substance dependence undermining emotional engagement
One partner has already emotionally disengaged and decided to leave
Severe personality pathology that prevents any emotional engagement
When one partner uses therapy sessions to further abuse or control the other

psychodynamic.name: Psychodynamic Therapy
psychodynamic.description: Explores unconscious processes, early relational patterns, and defense mechanisms to increase self-awareness and resolve deep-seated emotional conflicts.

sections.title: Overview
paragraphs: Psychodynamic therapy has its roots in Freudian psychoanalysis but has evolved significantly through object relations theory (Klein, Winnicott, Fairbairn), self psychology (Kohut), relational psychoanalysis (Mitchell, Aron), and mentalization-based approaches (Fonagy, Bateman).

The central premise is that much of mental life is unconscious, and current difficulties are shaped by early relational experiences, internalized object relations, and habitual defense mechanisms. Symptoms are understood as compromises between conflicting wishes, fears, and internalized relational patterns.

Modern psychodynamic therapy emphasizes the therapeutic relationship as both a window into the client's relational world (transference) and a vehicle for change. Brief psychodynamic therapies (12-24 sessions) focus on a central relational theme, while longer-term work addresses characterological patterns.

sections.title: Case Conceptualization Lens
paragraphs: Psychodynamic conceptualization organizes clinical data around unconscious conflict, defense mechanisms, object relations, and the repetition of early relational patterns in current life. The therapist attends to what is said, what is not said, and how the therapeutic relationship mirrors the client's core relational dynamics.
items: Core conflictual relationship theme (CCRT): wish → response of other → response of self
Predominant defense mechanisms (mature vs. primitive)
Quality of object relations (self-representations, other-representations, linking affect)
Attachment patterns and internal working models
Developmental history and early relational experiences
Transference patterns — how the client relates to the therapist
Countertransference — the therapist's emotional responses as diagnostic data
Level of personality organization (neurotic, borderline, psychotic — per Kernberg)
Capacity for mentalization and reflective functioning
Repetition compulsion — how early patterns replay in current relationships
sections.callout: Example formulation: The client's early experience of an emotionally unavailable parent created an internalized expectation that attachment figures will be disappointing and unreliable (object relation). This produces a core conflict between longing for closeness and fear of rejection. The client defends against this vulnerability through intellectualization and emotional distancing (defense mechanisms), which creates the very relational distance they fear (repetition compulsion). In therapy, this manifests as engaging intellectually while avoiding emotional depth (transference). The therapeutic work targets increasing awareness of this pattern and experiencing a different relational outcome within the therapeutic relationship.

sections.title: Cultural & Context Lens
paragraphs: Psychodynamic therapy must attend to how culture shapes the unconscious, defense mechanisms, relational patterns, and the meaning of symptoms. Cultural identity is not separate from psychodynamic processes — it is woven into them.
The therapist must examine their own cultural countertransference and consider how power dynamics related to race, class, gender, and sexuality operate within the therapeutic relationship.
items: Consider how cultural context shapes what is repressed or defended against
Examine how systemic oppression becomes internalized (internalized racism, classism)
Attend to cultural transference and countertransference
Recognize that some 'defenses' may be adaptive survival strategies in oppressive contexts
Consider how cultural norms shape the expression and meaning of attachment patterns
Explore how intergenerational trauma transmits through family systems and unconscious processes

sections.title: Measurement-Based Care
paragraphs: While psychodynamic therapy has historically relied less on standardized measures, modern practice integrates measurement to track progress.

subsections.title: Recommended Instruments
items: Shedler-Westen Assessment Procedure (SWAP-200) — Clinician-rated personality assessment. Provides dimensional personality profile. Administer at intake and every 6 months.
Reflective Functioning Questionnaire (RFQ) — 8-item self-report measuring mentalization capacity. Administer monthly.
Inventory of Interpersonal Problems (IIP-32) — 32-item measure of interpersonal difficulties. Administer monthly.
Defense Style Questionnaire (DSQ-40) — 40-item measure of defense mechanism maturity. Administer at intake and every 3 months.
OQ-45 (Outcome Questionnaire) — 45-item general functioning measure. Score >63 indicates clinical distress. Administer every 2-4 weeks.
PHQ-9 / GAD-7 — Brief symptom measures for depression and anxiety. Administer every 2-4 weeks.

sections.title: Defense Mechanisms
paragraphs: Defense mechanisms are unconscious psychological strategies used to manage anxiety and protect the ego from threatening thoughts, feelings, or impulses. They range from primitive (less adaptive) to mature (more adaptive).

subsections.title: Primitive Defenses
items: Splitting — Seeing self or others as all good or all bad
Projection — Attributing one's own unacceptable feelings to others
Projective Identification — Projecting feelings onto another and behaving in ways that elicit those feelings in them
Denial — Refusing to acknowledge painful reality
Idealization/Devaluation — Alternating between seeing others as perfect or worthless
Dissociation — Disconnecting from thoughts, feelings, or identity

subsections.title: Neurotic Defenses
items: Repression — Pushing threatening thoughts out of awareness
Displacement — Redirecting emotions from a threatening target to a safer one
Reaction Formation — Expressing the opposite of a threatening impulse
Intellectualization — Using abstract thinking to distance from emotional content
Rationalization — Creating logical explanations for emotionally driven behavior
Undoing — Attempting to reverse or atone for unacceptable thoughts or actions

subsections.title: Mature Defenses
items: Sublimation — Channeling unacceptable impulses into socially valued activities
Humor — Using comedy to express difficult feelings
Altruism — Meeting one's own needs through helping others
Suppression — Consciously choosing to postpone attention to a feeling or thought
Anticipation — Planning for future discomfort in realistic ways

sections.title: Key Psychodynamic Techniques
items: Free Association — Encouraging the client to say whatever comes to mind without censoring
Interpretation — Offering hypotheses about unconscious meaning, linking past patterns to present behavior
Clarification — Helping the client articulate vague or confused experiences more clearly
Confrontation — Gently drawing attention to contradictions, avoidances, or patterns the client may not see
Working Through — Repeatedly examining a core conflict or pattern across different contexts until insight becomes integrated
Transference Analysis — Exploring how the client's feelings toward the therapist reflect earlier relational patterns
Countertransference Use — Using the therapist's own emotional reactions as diagnostic information about the client's relational world
Dream Analysis — Exploring dream content as a window into unconscious processes
Linking Past and Present — Connecting current relational patterns to developmental experiences
Holding and Containment — Providing a safe therapeutic environment that can tolerate and metabolize difficult emotions

sections.title: When to Use / When NOT to Use

subsections.title: Best Fit
items: Chronic relational difficulties and repeating patterns
Personality disorders and characterological issues
Complex trauma with relational impact
Treatment-resistant depression or anxiety
Identity confusion and self-esteem difficulties
Clients seeking deeper self-understanding beyond symptom relief
Grief and loss with complicated mourning

subsections.title: Cautions / Contraindications
items: Active crisis requiring immediate stabilization (use crisis intervention first)
Severe cognitive impairment limiting capacity for self-reflection
Client preference for structured, skills-based approach
When a brief, evidence-based protocol exists for the specific presenting problem (e.g., specific phobia)
Active psychosis without adequate stabilization
When the client's primary need is psychoeducation or concrete skill-building
